nodes.label: Class Weights
nodes.summary: Class weights are a technique used in machine learning to address class imbalance by assigning higher importance to the minority class and lower importance to the majority class during model training. This approach modifies the loss function so that misclassifying a minority class sample incurs a higher penalty, forcing the model to pay more attention to under-represented patterns without generating synthetic data or removing samples. The most common method is Inverse Class Frequency, where the weight for each class is inversely proportional to its frequency in the dataset. Implementing class weights allows models to achieve better recall and AUC scores for the minority class, though this often comes at the cost of reduced precision and overall accuracy.

nodes.label: Post-Training Quantization
nodes.summary: Post-training quantization (PTQ) reduces model precision (e.g., FP32 to INT8) after training is complete, without requiring retraining. It calibrates quantization parameters using a small representative dataset, offering significant speedup and memory reduction with minimal accuracy loss. PTQ is the fastest path to deploying compressed models.

nodes.label: Quantization-Aware Training
nodes.summary: Quantization-aware training (QAT) simulates low-precision arithmetic during training by inserting fake quantization nodes into the computation graph. The model learns to compensate for quantization error, typically achieving higher accuracy than post-training quantization at the cost of a full training or fine-tuning run.

nodes.label: GPTQ
nodes.summary: GPTQ is a one-shot post-training quantization method for large language models that uses approximate second-order information (Hessian-based) to quantize weights to 3-4 bits with minimal perplexity loss. It processes weights column by column, using the inverse Hessian to optimally distribute quantization error across remaining weights.

nodes.label: AWQ
nodes.summary: Activation-aware Weight Quantization (AWQ) is a post-training quantization method that identifies salient weight channels by observing activation magnitudes rather than weight magnitudes. By protecting the 1% of weights most important to activations, AWQ achieves better quality than round-to-nearest quantization at 4-bit precision for large language models.

nodes.label: GGUF

nodes.label: Elastic Weight Consolidation
nodes.summary: Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) is a regularization technique for continual learning that mitigates catastrophic forgetting by selectively constraining important parameters. It uses the Fisher information matrix to estimate each parameter's importance to previously learned tasks, then adds a quadratic penalty to the loss function that discourages changes to critical weights while allowing less important ones to adapt to new tasks.
